source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, in $\triangle ABC$, $\angle BAC=90^\circ$, squares are constructed externally on sides $AB$, $CA$, $BC$. The area of square $ABIH$ is $25$, and the area of square $BDEC$ is $169$. What is the area of square $ACFG$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} In the right triangle $ABC$, $\angle BAC=90^\circ$, \\
$\therefore AB^2+AC^2=BC^2$, \\
$\because$ the area of the square $ABIH$ is $25$, and the area of the square $BDEC$ is $169$, \\
$\therefore AB^2=25, BC^2=169$, \\
$\therefore AC^2=BC^2-AB^2=169-25=144$, \\
$\therefore$ the area of the square $ACFG$ is $AC^2=\boxed{144}$.

however, the question text itself names the triangle, right angle, and the three squares with their areas, so the core computation appears largely recoverable from text alone
multi_step: solving requires connecting square area to side-length squared on AB and BC
the answer uses the right-triangle relation AB^2 + AC^2 = BC^2 and then computes AC^2 = 169 - 25
this is more than direct lookup: it combines geometric interpretation with a theorem and a final arithmetic step
verifiability: target answer is a single numeric value (144), which is easy to check
the provided solution gives an explicit derivation that can be stepwise verified from the stated quantities
